name: Qatari Arabic

ancestors.notes: Gulf Arabic base — inherits *q→[ɡ], velar affrication, interdental retention and emphatic spreading.

notes: Qatari Arabic. Inherits the Gulf features: *q → [ɡ]; velar affrication /k/→[tʃ] and /ɡ/→[dʒ] before high front /i, iː/; interdentals /θ ð ðˤ/ retained (conservative). The inherited affrication rules are conditioned on high front /i, iː/ precisely to match Mustafawi's (2006, 2007) Qatari analysis, reported in Alshammari 2026 p.1335: 'the velar stop undergoes affrication only in the stem when adjacent to the high front vowels /i/ or /i:/'. Mustafawi further documents that affrication is blocked by [-high] segments, by adjacent emphatics, and by the OCP (near an identical/similar coronal), and is systematically absent from broken plurals, verbal nouns, verbs and participles (paradigmatic levelling) — these blocking environments are NOT modelled in the base rule and remain an engine limit. Somewhat stronger tribal/Bedouin character than urban Kuwaiti. Input contract: fully diacritised (tashkeel) Arabic.

sources.author: Alshammari, K. K.
sources.title: An Optimality-Theoretic Account of Velar Affrication in Northern Najdi Arabic
sources.publisher: Journal of Language Teaching and Research 17(4)
sources.notes: Read in full (open-access PDF). Reports (p.1335) Mustafawi's (2006, 2007) Optimality-Theoretic account of Qatari velar affrication: /k/,/ɡ/ affricate only adjacent to high front /i, iː/; blocked by [-high] segments, emphatics, and the OCP; absent from broken plurals/verbal nouns/verbs/participles.

sources.author: Al-Balushi, R.
sources.title: Omani Arabic: More than a Dialect
sources.notes: Read the phonology sections. Cross-reference for Eastern Arabian interdental/emphatic retention and *q reflexes.
